pattern: Counting how many times each value occurs turns 'is this number isolated' into a cheap lookup: a value qualifies only when its own count is one and neither of its neighbors shows up at all.
hints: Start by tallying how many times each distinct value appears in the array.
A candidate value can only qualify if its own tally is exactly one -- anything appearing twice or more is disqualified immediately.
For every value whose tally is one, check the tally map for value-1 and value+1; if both are absent (zero), it's lonely.

edge_inputs.description: two adjacent-valued elements cancel each other out
unique value but a neighbor value also present elsewhere
value repeated twice is disqualified even though no neighbor exists
smallest possible value, zero, at the low end of the range
largest possible value at the high end of the range
no element qualifies, every value has a duplicate or neighbor
all elements identical, none lonely since count is not one

stress.description: large sparse array on a fixed-step lattice so every value stays lonely, order shuffled
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    nums = [i * 3 for i in range(n)]
    rng.shuffle(nums)
    return [nums]

stress.description: large dense random array over a small value range, forces many collisions and neighbor conflicts
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    nums = [rng.randint(0, 200) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]
